title: Machine Learning Fundamentals for Urban Planners
description: Learn ML concepts through real urban planning scenarios - choose to start with theory or hands-on practice

nodes.title: What is Machine Learning in Urban Planning?
nodes.content: Machine learning is teaching computers to learn patterns from data without being explicitly programmed. In urban planning, ML helps cities predict traffic patterns, optimize public transit routes, forecast housing demand, and identify optimal locations for new infrastructure. Instead of manually setting rules, planners feed historical data to ML models that automatically discover patterns—like how weather, events, and population changes affect transportation needs. This transforms urban planning from reactive guesswork into data-driven decision-making.

nodes.title: Choose Your Learning Path
nodes.content: How do you prefer to learn? Start with the mathematical foundations and algorithms, then see them in action? Or jump into real-world application and learn the theory as you go?

choices.label: Theory First - Learn algorithms and math foundations
Hands-On First - Build with data and learn concepts practically

nodes.title: ML Algorithms & Mathematical Foundations
nodes.content: Machine learning algorithms fall into three categories: supervised learning (learning from labeled examples), unsupervised learning (finding hidden patterns), and reinforcement learning (learning through rewards). For urban planning, supervised learning predicts outcomes like housing prices from location features, unsupervised learning clusters neighborhoods by similarity patterns, and reinforcement learning optimizes systems like traffic light timing. Key math concepts include features (inputs like latitude, longitude, population density), models (mathematical functions mapping inputs to outputs), and loss functions (metrics measuring prediction error). The training process iteratively adjusts the model to minimize error on historical data.

nodes.title: Predicting Urban Density Growth: A Worked Example
nodes.content: A city uses supervised learning to predict population density in new neighborhoods. Training data includes: location coordinates, proximity to metro stations, school district ratings, hectares of green space, and historical population growth rates. The model learns that neighborhoods within 500m of transit stations with top-rated schools experience 3-5% annual growth, while isolated areas grow 0.5% annually. Testing on new areas, it predicts neighborhood X will reach 8,000 people/sq-km within 5 years, guiding infrastructure investment decisions. This demonstrates feature engineering (selecting relevant variables), model training (learning relationships), and prediction (applying to new data).

nodes.title: Theory Path Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of ML fundamentals and algorithms.

questions.question: A city wants to predict optimal locations for new bike-share stations based on historical ridership data, neighborhood income levels, and proximity to bus transit hubs. Which type of learning is this?
options: Unsupervised learning - finding patterns without predefined categories
Supervised learning - predicting outcomes from labeled historical examples
Reinforcement learning - optimizing decisions through reward systems
Descriptive learning - summarizing existing data patterns
feedback: Not quite - we have labeled examples of past successful and unsuccessful station locations
Correct! We're predicting station success (labels) from features like income and transit proximity using historical data.
No - reinforcement learning optimizes sequential decisions over time, not single predictions
Not a standard ML approach for this problem
questions.explanation: This is supervised learning because we have historical examples of successful bike-share stations (positive labels) and unsuccessful locations (negative labels), and we want to predict outcomes for new candidate sites.

questions.question: A city government wants to cluster neighborhoods into distinct groups based on demographic patterns, housing types, average income, and land use—without predefining category labels. What learning type is this?
options: Supervised learning with regression models
Supervised learning with classification models
Unsupervised learning - clustering analysis
Reinforcement learning with exploration strategies
feedback: No - regression predicts continuous numbers, not groupings
No - classification requires pre-defined categories and labeled training data
Correct! Clustering discovers natural groupings in data without predefined category labels.
No - reinforcement learning is for sequential decision-making and optimization
questions.explanation: Clustering is unsupervised learning because neighborhoods aren't pre-labeled into groups; the algorithm discovers natural groupings based on similarity patterns in demographic and housing data.

questions.question: In the worked example about predicting urban density growth, what are 'features'?
options: The different ML algorithms available for prediction
Input variables like location coordinates, school ratings, and proximity to transit stations
The prediction the model makes (future population density)
The accuracy measurement and error rate of the trained model
feedback: No - algorithms are methods, not input data
Correct! Features are input variables that the model uses to learn patterns and make predictions.
No - that's the target variable or output we're predicting
No - that's a performance metric used to evaluate the model
questions.explanation: Features are the input variables—the specific data points you feed into the model. The model learns how features (location, school quality, transit proximity) relate to the target outcome (population density growth).

nodes.title: Building Your First ML Model: Practical Workflow
nodes.content: The ML workflow has four essential steps: (1) **Data Collection & Preparation** - gather historical urban data (traffic counts, pedestrian volumes, weather, events), handle missing values, and normalize numbers to consistent scales; (2) **Feature Engineering** - decide which variables matter most (distance to downtown core, population density, road width, zoning type) and create new meaningful features; (3) **Model Training** - feed prepared data to an algorithm that discovers patterns and builds a mathematical model; (4) **Evaluation & Deployment** - test accuracy on unseen data, compare against benchmarks, and if performance is acceptable, deploy for real predictions in production. For a traffic planner predicting commute times, you'd collect GPS trajectories from commuters, engineer features like distance traveled and rush-hour status, train on 80% of historical data, test on 20%, and deploy for real-time commute estimates.

nodes.title: Building a Transit Demand Predictor: Hands-On Example
nodes.content: A metropolitan transit authority builds an ML model to predict bus ridership for dynamic route planning and vehicle allocation. Step 1: Collect 2 years of daily ridership counts per route, weather conditions, temperature, precipitation, day-of-week, holidays, and major local events. Step 2: Engineer features like "rainy morning rush hour," "proximity to business district," "major sporting event scheduled," and "school holiday period." Step 3: Split data—train on 70% of daily observations (500+ days), validate on 15%, test on 15%. Step 4: Evaluate—the model achieves 87% accuracy on test data, exceeding the 80% threshold. Step 5: Deploy the model to predict next day's ridership, enabling the authority to schedule appropriate bus capacity and optimize driver shifts based on predicted demand patterns.

nodes.title: Hands-On Path Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of the ML workflow, practical implementation, and real-world deployment.

questions.question: A city planner has 10 years of traffic volume data including vehicle counts, weather conditions, time-of-day, and accident reports. They use 8 years for model training and 2 years for testing. What is the primary purpose of this train-test split?
options: Training uses less data for faster computation; testing validates accuracy on the full dataset
Training teaches the model to recognize patterns; testing evaluates performance on completely new, unseen data
Training prevents overfitting; testing allows overfitting to measure maximum possible accuracy
Training is a required step; testing is optional to save computation time
feedback: Not quite - both datasets should be equally important for reliable evaluation
Correct! The model learns from training data; testing on separate data measures how well it generalizes to new traffic patterns.
Reversed - testing on new data actually prevents overfitting conclusions
Testing is essential, not optional, for validating that a model works on real-world data
questions.explanation: Train-test splits (typically 70/30 or 80/20) ensure the model hasn't simply memorized training data patterns. Testing on completely separate temporal data reveals whether the model learned generalizable traffic patterns that work on new days and conditions.

questions.question: When engineering features for predicting optimal locations for new neighborhood parks, a team includes: distance to nearest schools, current population density, proximity to bus stops, percentage of green space in area, and average household income. What critical information is missing for real deployment?
options: The specific machine learning algorithm type (neural network, decision tree, etc.)
Zoning regulations, land availability, acquisition costs, and community engagement readiness
Weather patterns and seasonal variations in park usage
Historical data on park utilization and maintenance costs
feedback: The algorithm choice comes after good features are selected; not a blocker
Correct! ML discovers data patterns, but real decisions require feasibility constraints—land must be zoned appropriately, available, affordable, and aligned with community priorities.
Weather is useful data but not blocking; parks work in all seasons
Historical costs inform project ROI but aren't blocking to initial site prediction
questions.explanation: Machine learning finds patterns in data, but urban planners must also consider implementation constraints: zoning compatibility, land availability and cost, community preferences, and environmental regulations. ML is a decision-support tool, not a complete solution without these domain factors.

questions.question: While testing a housing demand prediction model on 2 years of historical data, the model achieves only 58% accuracy, far below the 80% performance target needed for production deployment. What should the data science team try next?
options: Deploy immediately—any ML model is better than manual guessing for housing forecasts
Gather additional historical data, engineer new features, try different algorithms, or adjust model parameters and hyperparameters
Abandon the project—if predictions can't reach 80%, the problem is unsolvable
Lower the accuracy requirement to 58% to match the model's performance
feedback: 58% accuracy is often worse than random guessing; deploying bad predictions harms planning decisions
Correct! Iterate by improving data quality, creating better features, testing other algorithms, and tuning parameters until you achieve target performance.
Low initial accuracy is common; iteration improves results significantly in most cases
No - standards protect decision quality; lowering them masks an unreliable model
questions.explanation: Low accuracy indicates the current approach isn't capturing relevant patterns. Teams iterate through: acquiring more or cleaner data, engineering more informative features, trying different algorithms (regression, trees, ensembles), and tuning parameters until performance meets requirements before production deployment.

nodes.title: You've Mastered Machine Learning Fundamentals for Urban Planning!
nodes.content: Congratulations! You now understand supervised and unsupervised learning, the complete ML workflow from data collection through deployment, and how to apply these concepts to urban challenges like traffic prediction, neighborhood clustering, housing demand forecasting, and infrastructure optimization. Whether you chose the theory-first path (algorithms and mathematical foundations) or hands-on path (workflow and practical implementation), you're equipped to collaborate with data scientists and make data-informed planning decisions. Next steps: explore specific algorithms (decision trees, random forests, neural networks), learn implementation tools like Python and scikit-learn, or apply these concepts to a real urban planning problem in your community. Cities worldwide are using ML to build smarter, more equitable, and sustainable communities—you're now prepared to lead that transformation.
